q: Whom is Alice sitting next to at the start of the story?
a: her sister, on the bank

q: What annoys Alice about her sister's book?
a: it has no pictures or conversations

q: What unusual creature does Alice see run by her?
a: a White Rabbit with pink eyes

q: What does the White Rabbit say to itself?
a: Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!

q: What does the White Rabbit take out of its waistcoat-pocket?
a: a watch

q: Where does Alice follow the Rabbit?
a: down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge

q: What did the rabbit-hole turn into?
a: a tunnel that dipped suddenly down into a very deep well

q: What did Alice see lining the sides of the well?
a: cupboards, book-shelves, maps, and pictures hung upon pegs

q: What was on the jar Alice took down from a shelf?
a: the label 'ORANGE MARMALADE' — but it was empty

q: How far did Alice estimate she was falling toward the centre of the earth?
a: four thousand miles down

q: What is Alice's cat's name?
a: Dinah

q: Onto what does Alice land at the bottom of the well?
a: a heap of sticks and dry leaves

q: What does Alice find on the three-legged glass table?
a: a tiny golden key

q: How tall is the little door behind the curtain?
a: about fifteen inches high

q: What is on the label of the bottle Alice finds?
a: DRINK ME

q: What flavour did the DRINK ME bottle have?
a: a sort of mixed flavour of cherry-tart, custard, pine-apple, roast turkey, toffee, and hot buttered toast

q: How tall did the bottle make Alice?
a: ten inches high

q: What did the cake under the table say?
a: EAT ME — beautifully marked in currants

q: What effect did eating the cake have?
a: She remained the same size.

q: Why couldn't Alice reach the golden key after she shrank?
a: the key was on the glass table, too high above her, and the table-leg was too slippery to climb

q: What does Alice do when she can't reach the key?
a: She sits down and cries.

q: Why did Alice not drop the marmalade jar?
a: for fear of killing somebody underneath

q: What does Alice exclaim when she suddenly grows tall?
a: Curiouser and curiouser!

q: How tall did Alice grow this time?
a: more than nine feet high

q: What forms around Alice as she cries?
a: a large pool of tears, about four inches deep, reaching half down the hall

q: What is the White Rabbit carrying when he returns?
a: a pair of white kid gloves in one hand and a large fan in the other

q: Whom is the White Rabbit afraid of keeping waiting?
a: the Duchess

q: What multiplication mistake does Alice make?
a: four times five is twelve, and four times six is thirteen, and four times seven is—

q: What geography mistake does Alice make?
a: London is the capital of Paris, and Paris is the capital of Rome

q: What poem does Alice try to recite that comes out wrong?
a: How doth the little crocodile (instead of How doth the little busy bee)

q: What in the crocodile poem does the crocodile do?
a: Improves his shining tail and pours the waters of the Nile on every golden scale

q: Two of the children Alice considers she might be?
a: Ada (long ringlets) and Mabel (knows little)

q: What did Alice put on without realising?
a: one of the Rabbit's little white kid gloves

q: What was making Alice shrink?
a: the fan she was holding

q: Where does Alice fall after being too small?
a: into a pool of salt water — actually her own pool of tears

q: Why might the Mouse be a French mouse?
a: Alice guesses it came over with William the Conqueror

q: What French phrase does Alice say to the Mouse?
a: Où est ma chatte?

q: Why does the Mouse get angry at Alice?
a: because she keeps mentioning cats and dogs — her family always hated cats

q: Which animals end up swimming in the pool of tears?
a: a Duck, a Dodo, a Lory, an Eaglet, and several other curious creatures

q: Whom does the Mouse try to dry the animals with?
a: the driest thing he knows — the history of William the Conqueror

q: Which earls does the Mouse mention from his history lecture?
a: Edwin and Morcar, the earls of Mercia and Northumbria

q: Who is the patriotic archbishop in the Mouse's history?
a: Stigand, the patriotic archbishop of Canterbury

q: Whom did the archbishop go with to meet William?
a: Edgar Atheling

q: What does the Dodo propose to dry the animals?
a: a Caucus-race

q: How does a Caucus-race work?
a: A circular race-course is marked out; runners begin and stop when they like; after about half an hour everyone is dry.

q: What does the Dodo say about the winner of the Caucus-race?
a: Everybody has won, and all must have prizes.

q: Where do the prizes come from?
a: from Alice's pocket — a box of comfits, one a-piece all round

q: What is Alice's own prize?
a: her thimble — solemnly presented back to her

q: Whose tale is described as 'long and sad'?
a: the Mouse's

q: What does Alice mishear about the Mouse's tale?
a: She thinks it is a long tail, not a long tale, and is puzzled why he calls it sad.

q: What is the name in the Mouse's tale who decides to be both judge and jury?
a: Fury — a cunning old dog/cur

q: Why does the Mouse leave?
a: Alice mentions Dinah's prowess in catching mice and birds, offending the whole party.

q: Who is left alone after the animals leave?
a: Alice

q: What name does the White Rabbit call Alice when he sees her hunting about?
a: Mary Ann (his housemaid)

q: What does the brass plate on the Rabbit's door say?
a: W. RABBIT

q: What does Alice drink in the Rabbit's house?
a: an unlabeled little bottle near the looking-glass

q: Describe what happened when Alice drank it.
a: She grew so large her head pressed against the ceiling, with one arm out the window and one foot up the chimney — filling the room.

q: Whom does the Rabbit call to help him?
a: Pat — who was 'digging for apples'

q: What does Alice's arm out the window look like to the Rabbit?
a: It fills the whole window — too large to be an arm

q: Who is sent down the chimney?
a: Bill the Lizard

q: What does Alice do to Bill?
a: Gives one sharp kick — Bill goes 'up like a sky-rocket'

q: What does the Rabbit threaten to do to the house?
a: burn it down — Alice retorts she will set Dinah at them

q: What does the Rabbit's gang throw in at the window?
a: a barrowful of little pebbles, which turn into little cakes when they hit the floor

q: What happens when Alice eats one of the cakes?
a: She begins shrinking — small enough to escape out the door

q: Where is poor Bill the Lizard when Alice runs out?
a: in the middle of a crowd, being held up by two guinea-pigs giving him something out of a bottle

q: What enormous animal does Alice meet in the wood?
a: an enormous puppy, looking down at her with large round eyes

q: How does Alice escape the puppy?
a: She dodges behind a great thistle while the puppy plays with a stick — then runs off when it tires.

q: What is on top of the large mushroom?
a: a large blue caterpillar, with arms folded, smoking a long hookah

q: What does the Caterpillar smoke?
a: a long hookah

q: What is the Caterpillar's first question to Alice?
a: Who are YOU?

q: What poem does the Caterpillar tell Alice to recite?
a: You are old, Father William

q: What does Father William do that worries his son?
a: stands on his head, finishes a goose with bones and beak, balances an eel on the end of his nose, and turns back-somersaults at his age

q: What ointment does Father William sell?
a: an ointment that keeps his limbs supple — one shilling the box

q: How does Father William end his exchange with his son?
a: I have answered three questions, and that is enough... Be off, or I'll kick you down stairs!

q: How tall is the Caterpillar exactly?
a: exactly three inches high

q: What does the Caterpillar reveal about the mushroom?
a: One side will make you grow taller, and the other side will make you grow shorter.

q: What happens when Alice nibbles the right-hand bit?
a: Her chin is pressed so closely against her foot — she shrinks dramatically.

q: What happens when she nibbles the left-hand bit?
a: Her neck stretches enormously, like a serpent rising out of a sea of green leaves.

q: Whom does Alice meet with the long neck?
a: an angry Pigeon, who mistakes her for a serpent

q: Where had the Pigeon tried to hide its eggs from serpents?
a: the roots of trees, banks, hedges, and finally the highest tree in the wood

q: What does the Pigeon believe little girls are?
a: a kind of serpent — because they eat eggs

q: How does Alice eventually return to her usual height?
a: by carefully nibbling first one then the other piece of mushroom

q: What does the Fish-Footman bring to the Frog-Footman?
a: a great letter, nearly as large as himself — an invitation from the Queen to play croquet

q: Describe the two Footmen.
a: Both are in livery with powdered hair curling all over their heads. One has a fish-face; the other has a frog-face with a round face and large eyes.

q: Who is sitting on the three-legged stool nursing a baby?
a: the Duchess

q: What is wrong with the kitchen's air?
a: too much pepper — making everyone sneeze (except the cook, and the grinning cat)

q: What kind of cat is grinning on the hearth?
a: a Cheshire cat

q: What does the Duchess say grinning cats are doing?
a: All Cheshire cats can grin, and most of them do.

q: What does the cook throw at the Duchess and baby?
a: fire-irons, then a shower of saucepans, plates, and dishes

q: What lullaby does the Duchess sing to the baby?
a: Speak roughly to your little boy, And beat him when he sneezes

q: What does the baby turn into?
a: a pig

q: What does Alice do with the pig?
a: Sets it down and lets it trot quietly away into the wood — 'It would have made a dreadfully ugly child.'

q: Where does the Cheshire Cat first reappear to Alice?
a: sitting on a bough of a tree a few yards off

q: What does the Cheshire Cat say everyone here is?
a: We're all mad here. I'm mad. You're mad.

q: How does the Cat prove a dog is sane and itself is mad?
a: A dog growls when angry, wags tail when pleased; the Cat growls when pleased and wags when angry — therefore mad.

q: Whom does the Cat tell Alice about as nearby residents?
a: the Hatter (one direction) and the March Hare (the other) — both mad

q: Why does Alice choose to visit the March Hare first?
a: She has seen hatters before, and as it is May the March Hare won't be raving mad — at least not so mad as it was in March.

q: How does the Cheshire Cat famously vanish?
a: slowly, beginning with the end of the tail, and ending with the grin which remained some time after the rest of it had gone

q: What is Alice's reflection on the vanishing grin?
a: I've often seen a cat without a grin, but a grin without a cat! It's the most curious thing I ever saw in my life!

q: What identifies the March Hare's house from a distance?
a: the chimneys were shaped like ears and the roof was thatched with fur

q: Who is having tea at the table under the tree?
a: the March Hare and the Hatter, with a Dormouse asleep between them

q: How is the Dormouse used?
a: as a cushion — the other two rest their elbows on it and talk over its head

q: What do the tea-party trio shout when they see Alice coming?
a: No room! No room!

q: What is the Hatter's famous riddle?
a: Why is a raven like a writing-desk?

q: Does the Hatter know the answer to his own riddle?
a: No — 'I haven't the slightest idea,' said the Hatter.

q: What is unusual about the Hatter's watch?
a: It tells the day of the month but not the time of day.

q: Why does the Hatter say the watch is two days wrong?
a: Because the March Hare put butter in the works (with bread-knife crumbs).

q: What concert is the Hatter remembering?
a: the great concert given by the Queen of Hearts, where he sang 'Twinkle, twinkle, little bat!'

q: How do the Twinkle, Twinkle parody lyrics go?
a: Twinkle, twinkle, little bat! / How I wonder what you're at! / Up above the world you fly, / Like a tea-tray in the sky.

q: What did the Queen say about the Hatter's singing?
a: He's murdering the time! Off with his head!

q: Why is it always tea-time at the Hare's table?
a: Time has refused to do anything the Hatter asks since the Queen sentenced him — 'It's always six o'clock now.'

q: What does the Dormouse begin telling about three little sisters?
a: Their names were Elsie, Lacie, and Tillie, and they lived at the bottom of a well.

q: What did Elsie, Lacie, and Tillie live on?
a: treacle

q: What kind of well did they live at the bottom of?
a: a treacle-well

q: What were the three sisters learning to draw?
a: Things beginning with M — mouse-traps, the moon, memory, and muchness

q: How does Alice leave the tea-party?
a: She walks off in great disgust — the Dormouse falls asleep instantly; they were trying to put it into the teapot as she left.

q: What does Alice find at the end of her walk away from the tea?
a: A tree with a door leading right into it — taking her back to the long hall.

q: What are the three gardeners doing to the rose-tree?
a: painting the white roses red

q: What are the gardeners' names?
a: Five, Seven, and Two — they are playing cards

q: Why are they painting the roses?
a: They had put the wrong (white) rose-tree in by mistake; if the Queen finds out, they will have their heads cut off.

q: What gets Seven in trouble?
a: bringing the cook tulip-roots instead of onions

q: Describe the Queen's procession.
a: Ten soldiers (clubs), ten courtiers (diamonds), ten royal children (hearts), guests including kings and queens, the White Rabbit, the Knave of Hearts carrying the King's crown, and finally the King and Queen of Hearts.

q: What is the Queen's response to almost everything?
a: Off with his/her head!

q: What are the croquet-balls?
a: live hedgehogs

q: What are the croquet mallets?
a: live flamingoes

q: What serve as the croquet arches?
a: the soldiers, doubled up to make arches

q: Who reappears in the air during the croquet game?
a: the Cheshire Cat — first as a grin, then eyes, then ears, then the whole head

q: What does the King think of the Cheshire Cat?
a: I don't like the look of it at all — but it may kiss my hand if it likes.

q: What does Alice say when the King wants the Cat removed?
a: A cat may look at a king.

q: What is the executioner's argument about beheading the Cat?
a: You can't cut off a head unless there is a body to cut it off from — he's never had to do such a thing before, and isn't going to begin at his time of life.

q: What is the King's argument?
a: anything that has a head can be beheaded

q: How does the Cat escape execution?
a: Its head fades away while the King and executioner search for the rest of its body.

q: Whom does Alice walk arm-in-arm with at the start of chapter 9?
a: the Duchess — who is in a much pleasanter temper than in the kitchen

q: What does the Duchess insist of everything?
a: Everything's got a moral, if only you can find it.

q: What is one Duchess moral?
a: Tis love, 'tis love, that makes the world go round!

q: What is another Duchess moral?
a: Take care of the sense, and the sounds will take care of themselves.

q: What is the Duchess's strange tangled moral?
a: Be what you would seem to be — never imagine yourself not to be otherwise than what it might appear to others that what you were or might have been was not otherwise than what you had been would have appeared to them to be otherwise.

q: What does the Queen say to the Duchess that makes her vanish?
a: Either you or your head must be off, and that in about half no time! Take your choice!

q: Whom does the Queen send Alice to see?
a: the Mock Turtle, accompanied by a Gryphon

q: What does the Gryphon say about the Queen's executions?
a: It's all her fancy, that: they never executes nobody, you know.

q: What is the Mock Turtle's first revelation?
a: Once I was a real Turtle.

q: Why did the Mock Turtle's class call their master Tortoise?
a: We called him Tortoise because he taught us.

q: What were the four branches of arithmetic at the Mock Turtle's school?
a: Ambition, Distraction, Uglification, and Derision

q: What other subjects did they study?
a: Reeling and Writhing; Mystery (ancient and modern); Seaography; Drawling, Stretching, and Fainting in Coils; Laughing and Grief

q: How many hours of lessons did they have on the first day?
a: ten hours

q: Why are they called lessons?
a: because they lessen from day to day

q: What 'extras' did the school's bill list?
a: French, music, AND washing — extra

q: Who taught Drawling, Stretching, and Fainting in Coils?
a: an old conger-eel that came once a week

q: Who taught Classics, and what?
a: an old crab who taught Laughing and Grief

q: How does the Lobster Quadrille begin?
a: Form into two lines along the sea-shore — seals, turtles, salmon — and clear away the jelly-fish

q: Who is each dancer's partner?
a: a lobster

q: What do you do with the lobsters in the dance?
a: Throw them as far out to sea as you can, swim after them, turn a somersault in the sea, change lobsters again, and back to land

q: Recite the start of the Whiting song.
a: Will you walk a little faster? said a whiting to a snail. There's a porpoise close behind us, and he's treading on my tail.

q: What does the Whiting song say about France?
a: What matters it how far we go? — There is another shore, you know, upon the other side. The further off from England the nearer is to France.

q: Why is a whiting called a whiting?
a: It does the boots and shoes — boots and shoes under the sea are done with a whiting

q: What are sea-boots and sea-shoes made of?
a: soles and eels

q: What is the Mock Turtle's pun about porpoise?
a: If a fish came to me and told me he was going a journey, I should say 'With what porpoise?' (i.e., purpose)

q: How does the Lobster verse start in Alice's mistaken recital?
a: 'Tis the voice of the Lobster; I heard him declare, / 'You have baked me too brown, I must sugar my hair.'

q: Who shares a pie in the Owl-Panther verse?
a: the Owl and the Panther — the Panther took pie-crust, gravy, and meat; the Owl had the dish as its share

q: Recite the Beautiful Soup chorus.
a: Beautiful Soup, so rich and green, / Waiting in a hot tureen! / Who for such dainties would not stoop? / Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup!

q: What interrupts the Mock Turtle's song?
a: a cry of 'The trial's beginning!' from the distance

q: Who is on trial?
a: the Knave of Hearts — for stealing the Queen's tarts

q: Recite the accusation as the White Rabbit reads it.
a: The Queen of Hearts, she made some tarts, all on a summer day; The Knave of Hearts, he stole those tarts, and took them quite away!

q: Who serves as judge in the trial?
a: the King — wearing his crown over his great wig

q: How many jurors are in the jury-box?
a: twelve creatures (some animals, some birds)

q: Which juror is named?
a: Bill the Lizard

q: What does Alice secretly do to one juror?
a: She takes away the squeaking pencil belonging to Bill the Lizard.

q: What is the first witness, and what does he carry?
a: the Hatter — carrying a teacup in one hand and a piece of bread-and-butter in the other

q: What dates do the Hatter, March Hare, and Dormouse give?
a: Hatter: Fourteenth of March; March Hare: Fifteenth; Dormouse: Sixteenth

q: What happens to Alice during the Hatter's testimony?
a: She begins to grow larger again.

q: What does the Dormouse object to as Alice grows?
a: I wish you wouldn't squeeze so — I can hardly breathe.

q: Who is the second witness?
a: the Duchess's cook — carrying the pepper-box

q: What does the cook say tarts are made of?
a: Pepper, mostly.

q: What does the Dormouse interrupt with about tarts?
a: Treacle

q: What does the Queen do when the Dormouse interrupts?
a: Collar that Dormouse! Behead that Dormouse! Turn that Dormouse out of court! Suppress him! Pinch him! Off with his whiskers!

q: Who is called as the next witness at the chapter's end?
a: Alice

q: What does Alice accidentally do to the jury-box?
a: Tips it over with the edge of her skirt — upsetting all the jurymen onto the heads of the crowd below

q: How does Alice put Bill the Lizard back?
a: She accidentally puts him in head downwards — then sets him right.

q: What does Alice say she knows about the business?
a: Nothing — Nothing whatever

q: How does the King correct his use of 'important'?
a: He swaps it for 'unimportant' after the White Rabbit makes faces — then goes on muttering 'important — unimportant — unimportant — important' to himself.

q: What rule does the King invoke against Alice?
a: Rule Forty-two — All persons more than a mile high to leave the court

q: Does Alice agree the rule applies?
a: No — she says it's not a regular rule, the King invented it just now.

q: What does Alice say about the rule's number?
a: Then it ought to be Number One.

q: What new evidence does the White Rabbit produce?
a: a paper that turns out to be a set of verses — not a letter

q: Where does the King say the Knave should have signed?
a: He should have signed his name like an honest man — not signing makes the matter worse

q: Recite the opening of the Knave's mystery verses.
a: They told me you had been to her, And mentioned me to him: She gave me a good character, But said I could not swim.

q: What does Alice declare about the verses?
a: I don't believe there's an atom of meaning in it.

q: What does the King's instruction to the White Rabbit say?
a: Begin at the beginning, and go on till you come to the end: then stop.

q: Recite the Queen's most famous (backwards) order.
a: Sentence first — verdict afterwards.

q: What is Alice's reaction to the Queen's order?
a: Stuff and nonsense! The idea of having the sentence first!

q: What does Alice say to the entire court?
a: You're nothing but a pack of cards!

q: What happens to the pack of cards?
a: The whole pack rises up into the air and comes flying down upon her.

q: Where does Alice find herself when she wakes?
a: lying on the bank, with her head in the lap of her sister, who is gently brushing away dead leaves

q: What does Alice's sister tell her?
a: Wake up, Alice dear! Why, what a long sleep you've had!

q: Was it real?
a: No — it was a dream; her sister kissed her and told her to run in to her tea, it was getting late

q: What does Alice's sister do after Alice runs off?
a: She sits still, leaning her head on her hand, watching the setting sun, and begins dreaming about Alice's adventures herself.

q: Why is Alice growing tired at the start?
a: She has nothing to do, and her sister's book has no pictures or conversations.

q: What does Alice consider doing on the bank?
a: making a daisy-chain

q: What does Alice threaten to set at the Rabbit's gang?
a: Dinah — her cat — if they burn the house down

q: What is the only piece of advice the Caterpillar gives initially?
a: Keep your temper.

q: What does Alice say about the baby once it has turned into a pig?
a: It would have made a dreadfully ugly child, but it makes rather a handsome pig.

q: What was the March Hare's excuse for the broken watch?
a: It was the best butter — but some crumbs must have got in as well, since the Hatter shouldn't have used the bread-knife.

q: What does the Queen do to the three gardeners?
a: Orders 'Off with their heads!' — Alice puts them in a large flower-pot to save them.

q: What is a Mock Turtle described as?
a: the thing Mock Turtle Soup is made from — sad, lonely, sitting on a little ledge of rock

q: What is the King's verdict on unsigned letters?
a: If you didn't sign it, that only makes the matter worse. You must have meant some mischief.

q: What does the Hatter compare the Dormouse-as-cushion to?
a: uncomfortable for the Dormouse — only, as it's asleep, it doesn't mind

q: What two things does Alice see that are remarkable about the rabbit?
a: It has a waistcoat-pocket and takes a watch out of it.

q: How does Alice address the Mouse politely?
a: O Mouse, do you know the way out of this pool?

q: What is the Dodo's pose during deliberation?
a: one finger pressed upon its forehead — the position you usually see Shakespeare in pictures of him

q: What does the Frog-Footman tell Alice about knocking?
a: There's no sort of use in knocking — he's on the same side of the door as her, and they're making such a noise inside no one could hear.

q: Why was the Duchess sentenced to execution?
a: She boxed the Queen's ears — she came rather late.

q: What does the Hatter accidentally bite during testimony?
a: a large piece out of his teacup, instead of the bread-and-butter

q: What does Alice say after the Pigeon attacks her?
a: I'm not a serpent! Let me alone!

q: What is the King's threat for nervous witnesses?
a: Give your evidence; and don't be nervous, or I'll have you executed on the spot.

q: What sleep-related interjection does the Dormouse make?
a: He says 'I breathe when I sleep' is the same thing as 'I sleep when I breathe' — to which the Hatter replies it IS the same thing for him.

q: Why is the croquet-ground so chaotic?
a: All ridges and furrows; balls (hedgehogs) crawl off; mallets (flamingoes) twist their necks; arches (soldiers) walk off; players play out of turn and quarrel.

q: What does the Mock Turtle say after a long pause when Alice is impatient?
a: When we were little, we went to school in the sea.

q: How does Alice's escape from the Rabbit's house begin?
a: She eats one of the cakes — pebbles that turned into cakes — and shrinks until she can fit through the door.

q: What does Alice do after eating the EAT ME cake?
a: Holds her hand on top of her head to feel which way she's growing — but remains the same size.

q: What does the cook keep doing while the Duchess sings?
a: Stirring the cauldron — and throwing things at the Duchess and baby.

q: What happens to the King's note-book at the end?
a: He shuts it hastily after Alice says the rule should be Number One — turning pale.

q: What does the King quietly say after the Queen takes Alice away?
a: You are all pardoned.

q: What does Alice address her right foot as?
a: Alice's Right Foot, Esq., Hearthrug, near the Fender, with Alice's love

q: How does the Hatter describe his confused testimony's start?
a: It began with the tea — 'twinkling of the tea' — and the King insists 'twinkling begins with a T.'

q: What does the Knave say about the unsigned verses?
a: I didn't write it, and they can't prove I did: there's no name signed at the end.

q: How does Alice find out the baby is a pig?
a: It grunts violently; she looks down and sees it has a turn-up nose like a snout, and tiny eyes for a baby.

q: What is the squeaking voice that comes after Bill is kicked?
a: Bill's own — 'something comes at me like a Jack-in-the-box, and up I goes like a sky-rocket!'

q: What had the Queen said to make Time stop working for the Hatter?
a: He's murdering the time! Off with his head!

q: Recite the snail's reply in the Whiting song.
a: Too far, too far! ... Said he thanked the whiting kindly, but he would not join the dance.

q: What does Alice's older sister begin to imagine after Alice leaves?
a: She pictures her little sister grown up, telling Wonderland stories to her own children — keeping the simple and loving heart of childhood.

q: What does Alice see in the loveliest garden through the little door?
a: beds of bright flowers and cool fountains

q: What does the Duchess say to the cook's flying utensils?
a: She takes no notice of them even when they hit her.

q: What did the Queen do when Alice said 'How should I know?'
a: Turned crimson with fury, glared like a wild beast, and screamed 'Off with her head! Off—'

q: What does the Mad Hatter mean when he says 'I see what I eat' is not the same as 'I eat what I see'?
a: The example is meant to show identity statements aren't reversible — you might say one but not mean the other.

q: What does the Duchess say about why she's not putting her arm around Alice's waist?
a: She's doubtful about the temper of the flamingo Alice is carrying — 'flamingoes and mustard both bite.'

q: What does Alice find she has only a 'thimble' to give to the Dodo?
a: When asked what else she had in her pocket, she says 'Only a thimble' — which the Dodo then ceremoniously presents back to her as her own prize.

q: What was the second-witness Cook's response to 'Give your evidence'?
a: Shan't.

q: What is Alice's age-status when she calls the court a pack of cards?
a: She has grown to her full size — no longer afraid.

q: What two things has Alice tried to recite by the Caterpillar's prompting?
a: How doth the little busy bee (came out as crocodile) and You are old, Father William

q: What does the Cheshire Cat reappear to ask Alice in the wood?
a: Did you say pig, or fig?

q: Why does Alice consider she might have been changed?
a: She doesn't know multiplication right or geography right; words to How Doth the Little come out wrong — perhaps she's been changed for Mabel.

q: What did Alice fear the puppy might do?
a: She was terribly frightened it might be hungry and eat her up in spite of all her coaxing.

q: Where do Alice and the Dormouse end up after the tea-party breakup?
a: She walks off; the others try to put the Dormouse into the teapot.

q: What is the unique feature of the Cheshire Cat's reappearance during croquet?
a: Just its head appears; the executioner argues he can't behead a head with no body.

q: What is the Gryphon's reassurance about the Mock Turtle's grief?
a: It's all his fancy, that: he hasn't got no sorrow, you know.

q: What does the Mock Turtle sing after the Quadrille?
a: Beautiful Soup — Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup!

q: What did the Hatter answer when asked the date?
a: The fourth — and the Hatter realized his watch was 'two days wrong'.

q: What does the King do with the note-book that he had been writing in?
a: He cackles 'Silence!' and reads out 'Rule Forty-two' from it — All persons more than a mile high to leave the court.

q: Who is described in the line about the uses of a book?
a: Alice — 'and what is the use of a book without pictures or conversations?'

q: What is the Hatter's view on Time as 'him'?
a: If you knew Time as well as I do, you wouldn't talk about wasting IT. It's HIM.

q: What had Alice studied about the earth's rotation that the Duchess interrupted?
a: Twenty-four hours, I think; or is it twelve?

q: What is the Duchess's moral about minding business?
a: It was supposed to be 'If everybody minded their own business, the world would go round a deal faster than it does' — first said in the kitchen — but the chapter-9 version is about love, sense, and morals.

q: How does the Mock Turtle pun on 'whiting' and shoes?
a: Whiting = the polish that does the boots and shoes under the sea; sea-shoes are made of soles and eels.

q: What does the Hatter trembling shake off?
a: Both his shoes.

q: What is Alice's height by the time of her final outburst?
a: her full size — she had grown back fully

q: What direction is the bottom of the rabbit-hole's hall?
a: long and low, lit up by a row of lamps hanging from the roof

q: Why did Alice believe she could shut up like a telescope?
a: So many out-of-the-way things had happened that she had begun to think very few things were really impossible.

q: Why does the Mock Turtle say the eleventh day was a holiday?
a: Because lessons lessen each day — ten on the first, nine the next — so the eleventh day there were no lessons.

q: What were Alice's missed dinner-tables before checking herself?
a: She begins 'I've often seen them at dinn—' (meaning dinner) but stops, and the Mock Turtle comments 'I don't know where Dinn may be.'

q: How does the verses-puzzle King justify 'they all returned from him to you'?
a: He points to the tarts on the table: 'Why, there they are!'

q: What do the Caterpillar's remarks consist of?
a: Very short remarks — terse, contradictory, often single-word answers like 'Why?' 'No' 'Not a bit'

q: Whom does the Mouse address with 'Our family always hated cats'?
a: Alice — defending its kind

q: What were the soldiers' reply when the Queen asked if heads were off?
a: Their heads are gone, if it please your Majesty!

q: What did Alice's older sister picture about Alice in the future?
a: How she would, in the after-time, be herself a grown woman, keeping the simple and loving heart of her childhood, and gathering other little children about her to tell them tales of Wonderland.

q: Why is the King's crown over his wig 'not at all becoming'?
a: Because he wears the crown over his great judge's wig, which doesn't look comfortable.

q: What does Alice consider doing once she's in the hall but is too small?
a: Going through the little door into the loveliest garden — but she can't reach the key.

q: What does Alice say as the Rabbit's gang plans?
a: She listens through the open door and overhears 'A barrowful will do, to begin with.'

q: How does the Hatter excuse the dropped teacup?
a: I'm a poor man, your Majesty — and I hadn't begun my tea — not above a week or so...

q: What feature of the King's verdict-getting attempts is repeated?
a: He keeps saying 'Consider your verdict' — 'for about the twentieth time that day' — but the trial keeps getting interrupted.

q: Where do tea-things keep being put?
a: they keep moving round the table — using up clean cups — never washing the things between whiles

q: What does the Duchess fling at Alice as she leaves to play croquet?
a: the baby

q: What did the Mock Turtle pun on about Drawling?
a: Drawling-master was an old conger-eel that taught Drawling, Stretching, and Fainting in Coils — a play on 'Drawing, Sketching, and Painting in Oils'.

q: What does Alice wonder about catching 'bats' as she falls?
a: Whether cats eat bats — 'Do cats eat bats?' or 'Do bats eat cats?'

q: What is the Gryphon's command to Alice about her adventures?
a: Come, let's hear some of YOUR adventures.

q: What makes Alice realize her tears are now in fact a pool she fell into?
a: She had been crying when nine feet high — she falls into the salt water, which turns out to be that very pool of tears.

q: What does the King say after silence about the verses' word 'fit'?
a: Then the words don't FIT you — said with a smile around the court — followed by a dead silence.

q: Why does Alice say 'I needn't be afraid of them'?
a: Because despite the Queen's procession, she realizes 'they're only a pack of cards, after all.'

q: What does Alice notice the jurors writing down without understanding?
a: Their own names — 'for fear they should forget them before the end of the trial' — and then 'stupid things!' after Alice's outburst.
